title: Single-Table Design & Multi-Value Indexes
description: The single-table design pattern invented for DynamoDB — made dramatically more powerful with Oracle's multi-value indexes, SQL joins, and cost-based optimizer.
introduction:
I invented single-table design at Amazon for DynamoDB. The idea was simple: co-locate related entities in one table using composite partition keys (pk) and sort keys (sk) so that a single query retrieves an entire entity graph — an advisor with all their clients, accounts, and holdings — in one round trip, one partition scan, zero joins. It was the right pattern for the right constraints. DynamoDB had no joins, no cost-based optimizer, no secondary indexes worth the name. You modeled for access patterns or you modeled for pain.

But here's what I've learned since: the pattern is powerful, and the platform was a prison. On DynamoDB, single-table design gives you fast point reads and range scans within a partition. That's it. Need to aggregate across partitions? Write a Lambda. Need to join entity types? Application-side stitching. Need to index into arrays? You can't — DynamoDB doesn't support it. Need a compound index on two array fields? Forget it. MongoDB has the same limitation — the infamous parallel arrays prohibition means you cannot create a compound index on two fields that are both arrays. The workarounds are ugly and the performance is worse.

On Oracle, the single-table design pattern keeps its locality benefits — pk/sk queries are fast, entity co-location works exactly as designed — but the prison walls disappear. You get SQL joins across entity types within the collection. You get GROUP BY, window functions, and CTEs for cross-partition analytics. You get functional indexes on JSON path expressions that work like DynamoDB GSIs but without the replication lag, storage duplication, or 20-GSI-per-table limit. And you get CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX — the ability to build full B-tree indexes on every element inside a JSON array, then query and combine them freely.



[svg:m3-single-table.svg]

In this module, you'll explore the advisory_entities collection where advisors, clients, accounts, and holdings co-exist using the pk/sk pattern. You'll create functional indexes for primary and alternate access patterns. You'll build multi-value indexes on tags and sectors arrays and combine them in queries that MongoDB physically cannot execute. You'll run SQL aggregation, CTEs with window functions, and JSON_TABLE shredding — analytical operations that would require an entirely separate database on any NoSQL platform. The pattern without the prison. That's the thesis.
checkpoint: One collection. Four entity types. Partition-key query returns an advisor with all their clients in a single round trip — plus SQL joins, aggregation, and multi-value indexes that DynamoDB and MongoDB cannot match.

exercises.title: Explore the Single-Table Collection
exercises.description: See all entity types in one collection, query by partition key, and use sort key prefixes.
exercises.explanation: This is the single-table design pattern I invented at Amazon for DynamoDB. The idea is simple but powerful: co-locate related entities in one collection using a **partition key (pk)** for grouping and a **sort key (sk)** for type filtering. The pk groups everything belonging to an advisor — clients, accounts, holdings — into one partition, so a single query retrieves the entire entity graph without joins or cross-partition scatter. The sk prefix (like 'CLIENT#') lets you filter by entity type within that partition, giving you selective access to just the slice you need. On DynamoDB you get this and nothing else — no ad-hoc queries, no aggregation, no joins across partitions. On Oracle, you get this plus the full power of SQL, joins, window functions, and a cost-based optimizer that has been battle-tested for 40 years — the pattern without the prison. The **entityType** field is critical: it lets you distinguish between advisors, clients, accounts, and holdings that all live in the same collection. **Try this:** Run `SELECT DISTINCT data.pk.string() FROM advisory_entities ORDER BY 1` to see all partition keys, or try `SELECT data.sk.string(), json_serialize(data.data PRETTY) FROM advisory_entities WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#3' AND data.sk.string() LIKE 'ACCOUNT#%'` to explore a different advisor's accounts.
exercises.code:
-- See all entity types in one collection
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities
GROUP BY data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

-- Query by partition key — get an advisor and all their entities
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS type,
       data.sk.string() AS sort_key,
       json_serialize(data.data PRETTY) AS payload
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
ORDER BY data.sk.string();

-- Query by sort key prefix — get just the clients for an advisor
SELECT data.data.firstName.string() || ' ' || data.data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       data.data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       data.data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
  AND data.sk.string() LIKE 'CLIENT#%'
ORDER BY data.data.totalValue.number() DESC;

steps.label: Count all entity types in the collection
steps.code:
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities
GROUP BY data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

steps.label: Query by partition key — get an advisor and all their entities
steps.code:
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS type,
       data.sk.string() AS sort_key,
       json_serialize(data.data PRETTY) AS payload
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
ORDER BY data.sk.string();

steps.label: Query by sort key prefix — get just the clients for an advisor
steps.code:
SELECT data.data.firstName.string() || ' ' || data.data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       data.data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       data.data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
  AND data.sk.string() LIKE 'CLIENT#%'
ORDER BY data.data.totalValue.number() DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT data.entityType.string()) AS type_count FROM advisory_entities
exercises.checkpoint: You just queried a single-table collection using partition key and sort key prefix patterns — the same access pattern that powered Amazon's retail operation. One query, multiple entity types.

exercises.title: Create Functional Indexes for Access Patterns
exercises.description: Create primary and GSI-equivalent indexes on the single-table collection and verify index usage.
exercises.explanation: In DynamoDB, you define Global Secondary Indexes (GSIs) to support alternate access patterns — and you pay for every one with provisioned capacity, storage duplication, and replication lag that can leave reads stale for seconds. Oracle's **functional indexes** on JSON path expressions give you the same access pattern support, but backed by real B-tree indexes and a **cost-based optimizer** that decides when to use them. CREATE INDEX on a dot-notation path like `data.pk.string()` is the Oracle equivalent of a DynamoDB GSI — except it is transactionally consistent, free of replication lag, and the optimizer can combine multiple indexes in a single query plan using bitmap conversion. The composite index on `(pk, sk)` supports the primary entity co-location pattern, while the GSI-equivalent index on `(gsi1pk, gsi1sk)` supports inverted access patterns like 'find all holdings for a given stock symbol.' You can create as many functional indexes as your access patterns demand without the DynamoDB limit of 20 GSIs per table. **Try this:** Run `SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY_CURSOR())` after one of the queries above to see the optimizer's execution plan, or try `SELECT data.gsi1pk.string(), COUNT(*) FROM advisory_entities WHERE data.gsi1pk.string() IS NOT NULL GROUP BY data.gsi1pk.string() ORDER BY 2 DESC` to see all GSI partition key values and their distribution.
exercises.code:
-- Primary access pattern: pk + sk (entity co-location)
CREATE INDEX idx_pk_sk ON advisory_entities (
    data.pk.string(),
    data.sk.string()
);

-- GSI equivalent: alternate access pattern
CREATE INDEX idx_gsi1 ON advisory_entities (
    data.gsi1pk.string(),
    data.gsi1sk.string()
);

-- Use the GSI to find all holdings for a symbol across all accounts
SELECT data.pk.string() AS account,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.gsi1pk.string() = 'SYMBOL#AAPL'
  AND data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Create composite index on pk + sk for entity co-location queries
Create GSI-equivalent index for alternate access patterns
Use the GSI index to find all holdings for a symbol
steps.code:
SELECT data.pk.string() AS account,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.gsi1pk.string() = 'SYMBOL#AAPL'
  AND data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_PK_SK', 'IDX_GSI1')
exercises.checkpoint: You just created functional indexes on JSON paths — the Oracle equivalent of DynamoDB GSIs, but backed by real B-trees with zero replication lag.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Indexes — Index Into Arrays
exercises.description: Create multi-value indexes on tags and sectors arrays, then query across both — something impossible on MongoDB due to the parallel arrays prohibition.
exercises.explanation: **CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX** lets Oracle build a B-tree index on every element inside a JSON array. Each array element gets its own index entry, so `JSON_EXISTS` queries on array contents resolve via fast B-tree lookup — not a full collection scan. This is critical for any domain with tags, categories, or multi-valued attributes (which is most real-world data). Here is the kicker: MongoDB prohibits compound indexes on two array fields — the infamous **parallel arrays restriction**. Oracle has no such limitation. You can create multi-value indexes on tags AND sectors and query both in the same WHERE clause without error. The optimizer uses **bitmap conversion** to intersect the two indexes efficiently, meaning a query like 'find all S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector' hits two B-tree indexes and merges the results without scanning a single document that does not match. The `JSON_EXISTS` function with the filter expression `?(@ == "dividend")` is the SQL/JSON path language for 'does this array contain a value equal to dividend.' **Try this:** Run `SELECT data.data.symbol.string(), json_serialize(data.data.tags) FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "growth")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'` to find growth-tagged holdings, or try combining three conditions: tags, sectors, and a market value threshold with `AND ae.data.data.marketValue.number() > 50000`.
exercises.code:
-- Create a multi-value index on the tags array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.tags.string());

-- Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.sectors.string());

-- Find all holdings tagged "dividend"
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(data.data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

-- Combine: find all S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the tags array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.tags.string());

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.sectors.string());

steps.label: Find all holdings tagged 'dividend'
steps.code:
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(data.data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Combine both indexes — find S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector
steps.code:
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_TAGS', 'IDX_SECTORS')
exercises.checkpoint: You just indexed into JSON arrays with MULTIVALUE INDEX and queried across two array fields in one WHERE clause. On MongoDB, this compound index would fail — parallel arrays prohibition. On Oracle, it's standard B-tree semantics.

exercises.title: SQL Superpowers on Single-Table Data
exercises.description: Cross-entity aggregation, tag analytics, and sector exposure reports — queries that single-table design on DynamoDB could never support.
exercises.explanation: **JSON_TABLE** is where document and relational worlds collide — and Oracle wins. It shreds JSON arrays into relational rows using a COLUMNS clause that maps JSON paths to typed SQL columns, turning nested array elements into first-class rows that participate in GROUP BY, SUM, window functions, and CTEs. The tag analytics query demonstrates this: each holding's tags array is exploded into individual rows, then grouped and aggregated across the entire book of business — a query that would require a multi-stage `$unwind` and `$group` pipeline in MongoDB with no cost-based optimization. The sector exposure report goes further: it uses a **CTE (Common Table Expression)** with `SUM() OVER()` as a window function to calculate each sector's percentage of total portfolio value in a single pass. This is exactly why convergence matters — you model as documents for developer velocity, query as relational for analytical power, and the optimizer figures out the fastest execution path. Note that JSON_TABLE performs an implicit lateral join, meaning each row in the outer table is joined with the rows generated from its own JSON array. **Try this:** Run `SELECT jt.tag, ae.data.pk.string() AS advisor, COUNT(*) FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag, ae.data.pk.string() ORDER BY 1, 2` to see tag distribution per advisor, or add `HAVING COUNT(*) > 2` to find tags that appear more than twice.
exercises.code:
-- TAG ANALYTICS: Holdings count by tag (across entire book)
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

-- SECTOR EXPOSURE REPORT: Market value by sector with percentage
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

steps.label: Tag analytics — holdings count and total value by tag
steps.code:
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

steps.label: Sector exposure report — market value by sector with portfolio percentage
steps.code:
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM (SELECT jt.tag FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag)
exercises.checkpoint: You just ran SQL aggregations across entity types using JSON_TABLE and window functions — queries that single-table design on DynamoDB could never support.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Index + Duality View — Best of All Worlds
exercises.description: Combine single-table design with duality views — multi-value index identifies matches, SQL JOIN navigates relationships, duality view shapes the output.
exercises.explanation: This is the convergence payoff — **three paradigms in one query**, one optimizer, one round trip. The multi-value index on the single-table collection identifies holdings tagged 'dividend' via B-tree lookup, avoiding any collection scan. The SQL JOIN navigates from the single-table entity to the relational clients table, bridging the document and relational models within the same transaction. The duality view then projects the final output as a rich JSON document with nested accounts and holdings — the shape your API consumer expects. On any other platform, this query would require three separate databases (a document store for the single-table collection, an RDBMS for client data, and an aggregation layer to stitch them together), three network round trips, and a prayer that nothing changed between the reads. Here it is one SQL statement and the cost-based optimizer decides whether to use nested loop joins, hash joins, or merge joins based on real-time statistics from both the collection and the relational tables. This pattern is the architectural thesis of the entire workshop: **model the domain, project the access, let the optimizer handle the physics**. **Try this:** Change the tag filter from 'dividend' to 'growth' or 'sp500' to see different client portfolios, or wrap the query in `SELECT json_serialize(client_portfolio PRETTY)` for formatted output that is easier to read.
exercises.code:
-- Query: Find clients whose holdings include "dividend" tagged securities
-- using the multi-value index on the single-table collection,
-- then join to the relational model for the full client document
SELECT json_serialize(cp.data PRETTY) AS client_portfolio
FROM advisory_entities ae
JOIN clients c ON c.client_id = TO_NUMBER(
    REPLACE(ae.data.pk.string(), 'ACCOUNT#', '')
)
JOIN client_portfolio_dv cp ON cp.json_value(data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = c.client_id
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
exercises.checkpoint: You just combined multi-value index lookup, SQL JOIN, and duality view projection in a single query. Three paradigms. One optimizer. One round trip.
